perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS “Affiliates” shall mean any person or entity directly or indirectly controlling, controlled by, or under common c ontrol with Euler Hermes, where control means the legal power to direct or cause the direction of the general mana gement of the c ompany, partnership , or other legal entity
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS “Affiliates” shall mean any person or entity directly or indirectly associated with Euler Hermes, where association means having a business relationship or shared interests, regardless of control or ownership.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of 'Affiliates' is broadened to include entities without actual control, conflicting with standard legal interpretations of corporate affiliation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 12b-2 defines 'affiliate' based on control, whereas the modified contract broadens the definition to include mere association, creating ambiguity and potential liability issues under securities law.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.12b-2 Definitions. Unless the context otherwise requires, the following terms, when used in the rules contained in this regulation or in Regulation 13A or 15D or in the forms for statements and reports filed pursuant to sections 12, 13 or 15(d) of the act, shall have the respective meanings indicated in this rule: Accelerated filer and large accelerated filer —(1) Accelerated filer. The term accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $75 million or more, but less than $700 million, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78m or 78o(d) ) for a period of at least twelve calendar months; and (iii) The issuer has filed at least one annual report pursuant to section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act; and (iv) The issuer is not eligible to use the requirements for smaller reporting companies under the revenue test in paragraph (2) or (3)(iii)(B) of the “smaller reporting company” definition in this section , as applicable. (2) Large accelerated filer. The term large accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $700 million or more, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) o
perturbation.justification: The original text defines "Affiliates" based on control, which aligns with the legal definition as suggested by the provided law explanation and the definition in 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 which deals with definitions in the context of securities regulations. The changed text broadens this definition to include entities with a mere 'business relationship or shared interests, regardless of control or ownership.' This contradicts the standard legal interpretation of 'affiliate' as described in the law explanation and implied by 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 which focuses on control in the context of defining accelerated and large accelerated filers, which relates to affiliates.

perturbation.original_text: Euler Hermes shall keep the Confidential Information confidential and may disclose the Confidential Information only to its Affiliates , employees, contractors , or consultants for the Purpose described above and no other purpose. Euler Hermes shall be liable for any breach of confidentiality by any Affiliate, employee, contractor , or consultant.
perturbation.changed_text: Euler Hermes shall endeavor to keep the Confidential Information confidential and may disclose the Confidential Information to its Affiliates, employees, contractors, or consultants. Euler Hermes is responsible for its own actions.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the explicit liability for breaches by Affiliates, employees, contractors, or consultants weakens the confidentiality protection, potentially contradicting data protection laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Maryland Personal Information Protection Act mandates reasonable security measures to protect personal information. By removing the direct liability for breaches by affiliates, employees, contractors or consultants, the contract potentially fails to provide the necessary level of protection required by the law. Specifically, this is a problem if the company shares Maryland residents personal information with Euler Hermes.

(a) In this subtitle the following words have the meanings indicated. (b) (1) “Business” means a sole proprietorship, partnership, corporation, association, or any other business entity, whether or not organized to operate at a profit. (2) “Business” includes a financial institution organized, chartered, licensed, or otherwise authorized under the laws of this State, any other state, the United States, or any other country, and the parent or subsidiary of a financial institution. (c) “Encrypted” means the protection of data in electronic or optical form using an encryption technology that renders the data indecipherable without an associated cryptographic key necessary to enable decryption of the data. (d) “Health information” means any information regarding an individual’s medical history, medical condition, or medical treatment or diagnosis. (e) (1) “Personal information” means: (i) An individual’s first name or first initial and last name in combination with any one or more of the following data elements, when the data elements are not encrypted, redacted, or otherwise protected by another method that renders the information unreadable or unusable: 1. A Social Security number, an Individual Taxpayer Identification Number, a passport number, or other identification number issued by the federal government; 2. A driver’s license number or State identification card number; 3. An account number, a credit card number, or a debit card number, in combination with any required security code, access code, or password, that permits access to an individual’s financial account; 4. Health information, including information about an individual’s mental health; 5. A health insurance policy or certificate number or health insurance subscriber identification number, in combination with a unique identifier used by an insurer or an employer that is self–insured, that permits access to an individual’s health information; 6. Biometric data of an individual generated by automatic mea
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces "shall be liable for any breach of confidentiality" with "is responsible for its own actions." This weakens the explicit liability of Euler Hermes for breaches by its affiliates, employees, contractors, or consultants, potentially contradicting the requirement for reasonable security measures to protect personal information under laws such as the Maryland Personal Information Protection Act. The original contract held Euler Hermes liable for actions of those it shares data with. The new contract does not. The provided snippet defines "personal information", but the Law Explanation argues that the modified contract potentially fails to provide the necessary level of protection required by the law (which is undermined by the change in liability), and this supports the presence of a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Euler Hermes shall protect the Confidential Information from disclos ure to any firm or individual other than as authorized in paragraph 1, above, by using the same degree of care, but no less than reasonable care, that it uses to protect its own confidential information of a similar nature . Euler Hermes will instruct its Affiliates, employees, contractors, or consultants that may have access to the Confidential Information to keep it confidential in compliance with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Euler Hermes should attempt to protect the Confidential Information from disclosure to any firm or individual other than as authorized in paragraph 1, above, by using care it deems appropriate. Euler Hermes may optionally instruct its Affiliates, employees, contractors, or consultants that may have access to the Confidential Information to keep it confidential in compliance with this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The requirement for 'reasonable care' is weakened to 'care it deems appropriate,' and the instruction to affiliates is made optional, reducing the protection of confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Trade Secrets Act (as adopted in Maryland)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Changing 'reasonable care' to 'care it deems appropriate' and making instructions to affiliates optional may be insufficient to meet the 'reasonable efforts' standard, potentially jeopardizing trade secret protection.

(a) Except to the extent that a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation renders a monetary recovery inequitable, a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. (b) Damages under this subtitle may include: (1) The actual loss caused by misappropriation; and (2) The unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. (c) In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator’s unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. (d) If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under subsection (a) of this section. Previous Next Validation Please fix the following: OK Click to return on the top page
perturbation.justification: The original contract requires Euler Hermes to protect confidential information with "reasonable care", but the changed contract only requires "care it deems appropriate". The original contract mandates instructing affiliates to keep information confidential, while the changed contract makes it optional. The law explanation notes that the Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Scraped Law Snippet 1 supports this by detailing damages for misappropriation, implying a need for reasonable preventative measures. The change from 'reasonable care' to a subjective standard and making instructions optional weakens these protections, increasing the risk of misappropriation and potentially contradicting the requirement of reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be exclusively governed by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of the state of Maryland, and the parties agree to submit to the jurisdiction of the courts of that state for the resolution of any claim or matter arising under this Agreement
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the state of Maryland, but the parties may elect to resolve disputes through arbitration in a location of their choosing.
perturbation.explanation: Introducing the possibility of arbitration outside of Maryland alters the exclusive jurisdiction clause, potentially conflicting with established jurisdictional rules.
perturbation.law_explanation: While arbitration is generally permissible, removing the *exclusive* forum selection clause of Maryland opens the door to federal diversity jurisdiction if the amount in controversy exceeds $75,000 and the parties are citizens of different states. This removes the guarantee of Maryland courts which was in the original contract.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1332 - Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1332 - Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The district courts shall have original jurisdiction of all civil actions where the matter in controversy exceeds the sum or value of $75,000, exclusive of interest and costs, and is between— (1) citizens of different States; (2) citizens of a State and citizens or subjects of a foreign state, except that the district courts shall not have original jurisdiction under this subsection of an action between citizens of a State and citizens or subjects of a foreign state who are lawfully admitted for permanent residence in the United States and are domiciled in the same State; (3) citizens of different States and in which citizens or subjects of a foreign state are additional parties; and (4) a foreign state, defined in section 1603(a) of this title , as plaintiff and citizens of a State or of different States. (b) Except when express provision therefor is otherwise made in a statute of the United States, where the plaintiff who files the case originally in the Federal courts is finally adjudged to be entitled to recover less than the sum or value of $75,000, computed without regard to any setoff or counterclaim to which the defendant may be adjudged to be entitled, and exclusive of interest and costs, the district court may deny costs to the plaintiff and, in addition, may impose costs on the plaintiff. (c) For the purposes of this section and section 1441 of this title — (1) a corporation shall be deemed to be a citizen of every State and foreign state by which it has been incorporated and of the State or foreign state where it has its principal place of business, except that in any direct action against the insurer of a policy or contract of liability insura
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces arbitration outside of Maryland, which contradicts the original agreement's exclusive jurisdiction clause specifying Maryland courts. The law explanation states that this change opens the door to federal diversity jurisdiction under 28 U.S.C. § 1332, as cited in Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2, if the amount in controversy exceeds $75,000 and the parties are citizens of different states. This undermines the original contract's guarantee of Maryland courts, creating a direct and serious contradiction.
